summary: Jeff Atwood shares the discovery that hardware engineers, like their software counterparts, hide Easter eggs in their work. The post highlights a collection of hidden art etched into microchips, featuring an example of Mr. T on a T1 transceiver chip. Atwood points readers to the Silicon Zoo website, which catalogs these microscopic artistic touches found in integrated circuits. The brief post celebrates the playful creativity that exists even in the most technical hardware engineering.
key_points: Hardware engineers embed hidden art and jokes into microchip designs, similar to software Easter eggs
The Silicon Zoo website documents a collection of these microscopic artworks found on integrated circuits
The example shown features Mr. T etched onto a T1 transceiver chip, with his medallion appropriately reading 'T1'
money_quotes: Software engineers aren't the only ones planting secret Easter eggs in their products.
Who said hardware engineers didn't have a sense of humor?
key_insight: The tradition of hiding Easter eggs and personal touches in technical work extends beyond software into the microscopic world of chip design, revealing a universal creative impulse among engineers.
